ontology.foodex2_description: Sweet rolls and buns — cinnamon roll type
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for cream-cheese-frosted cinnamon roll; A0CJL (sweet rolls/buns) used as closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept identified for cinnamon roll; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical medium cinnamon roll weighs approximately 100 g and represents a single bakery serving.

nutrient_source.title: Rolls, cinnamon, commercially prepared with raisins (USDA SR Legacy NDB 18027) and HPB Singapore Foodo cinnamon roll entry
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service (FoodData Central SR Legacy) and Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary macro values (energy 388 kcal, protein 7.27 g, fat 18.0 g, carbohydrate 49.12 g, sugar 18.7 g, sodium 321 mg, dietary fibre 2.22 g) are taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo record (confidence 0.95, reviewed). Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for commercially prepared cinnamon roll with cream cheese frosting (NDB 18027 / FDC ID 174987); saturated fat reported at approximately 8.5 g/100 g for this product type. Micronutrients not reported in HPB source are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — white bread and sweet baked goods analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for cream-cheese-frosted cinnamon roll in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 78 is estimated from the closest analogous entry: commercially prepared sweet white-flour bun / sweet roll (GI ~75–80, white bread reference). Glycemic load per 100 g serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate g per serving) / 100 = (78 × 49.12) / 100 ≈ 38.3. This is a high-GI, high-GL food consistent with its refined-flour and high-sugar composition.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar (18.7 g/100 g) driving insulin-mediated sodium retention, borderline sodium (321 mg/100 g), and estimated saturated fat ~8.5 g/100 g contributing to arterial stiffness
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit cinnamon rolls; the combination of high sugar, borderline sodium, and high saturated fat creates compounding cardiovascular risk, and the cream cheese frosting should be omitted where possible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 18.7 g/100 g exceeds the 15 g/100 g hard gate; carbohydrate at 49.12 g/100 g from refined white flour with only 2.22 g/100 g dietary fibre produces a very high glycaemic load (GL ≈ 38 per 100 g serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Cinnamon rolls are not suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; the high sugar content, very high glycaemic load, and low fibre content cause rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation and should be avoided or replaced with lower-GI whole-grain alternatives.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat ~8.5 g/100 g from butter and cream cheese frosting, trace trans fat ~0.2 g/100 g, and high sugar 18.7 g/100 g collectively elevate LDL cholesterol and triglycerides
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid cinnamon rolls; the high saturated fat from butter and cream cheese frosting, combined with trace trans fat and high sugar, adversely affects lipid profiles and increases atherosclerotic risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat ~8.5 g/100 g exceeds the 5 g/100 g RA threshold; trace trans fat ~0.2 g/100 g and sugar 18.7 g/100 g promote systemic inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid cinnamon rolls; the high saturated fat from butter and cream cheese frosting, trace trans fat, and high sugar content all promote pro-inflammatory mediators that can exacerbate joint inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A cinnamon roll is a classic American sweet pastry made from a soft, enriched yeast dough rolled with a generous filling of ground cinnamon, granulated sugar, and butter, then baked until golden and topped with a rich cream cheese frosting. The texture is pillowy and tender with a slightly chewy interior, while the frosting adds a creamy, tangy sweetness. Cinnamon rolls are commonly consumed as a breakfast item or indulgent snack and are widely available in bakeries, cafes, and fast-food chains across the United States and internationally. Nutritionally, a 100 g serving provides approximately 388 kcal, 7.27 g protein, 18.0 g fat (of which ~8.5 g is saturated), 49.12 g carbohydrate, 18.7 g sugar, 2.22 g dietary fibre, and 321 mg sodium. The high sugar content (18.7 g/100 g), very high glycaemic index (~78), and high glycaemic load (~38 per 100 g) make this food clinically unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. The refined white flour base, butter, and cream cheese frosting are the primary contributors to its high saturated fat and sugar load. Cinnamon rolls should be consumed only occasionally and in small portions by the general population.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a cinnamon roll?
Is cinnamon roll high in sugar?
Is cinnamon roll suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core macro values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo reviewed record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy commercially prepared cinnamon roll entry, which is the closest approved-source analogue. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous sweet white-flour bun entry. All values are internally consistent and cross-validated between HPB and USDA sources.
notes: Saturated fat (8.5 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 18027 (commercially prepared cinnamon roll with cream cheese frosting) as HPB source does not report saturated fat separately. Trans fat (0.2 g/100 g) is a trace estimate consistent with butter-containing baked goods per USDA SR Legacy data; label verification recommended. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 because detailed micronutrient data for this specific cream-cheese-frosted cinnamon roll formulation is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database, and the USDA SR Legacy entry does not provide a complete micronutrient panel for this exact product type as of 2026. GI of 78 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database sweet white-flour bun category; no direct GI measurement for cream-cheese-frosted cinnamon roll was found in the database. Halal status is not confirmed as commercial cinnamon rolls may use non-halal lard or gelatin in some formulations; marked false pending verification. This is a Western (American) pastry; no mandatory multilingual names apply under Project Veritas v2 multilingual rules (rules apply to Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, and Vietnamese dishes only).
